posts.title: Ansel Adams Manzanar Internment Camp Exhibit, 1943
posts.text:
In 1943, Ansel Adams drove to a detention camp in the California desert and started photographing the people locked inside.

Not landscapes. Not mountains. People. Japanese-Americans who'd lost their homes, businesses, and freedom after Pearl Harbor.

He shot over 200 photos at Manzanar. Portraits, mostly. Families eating in mess halls. Kids playing baseball in the dust. A man tending a garden in 100-degree heat.

When Adams offered the collection to the Library of Congress in 1965, he wrote: "The purpose of my work was to show how these people, suffering under a great injustice, had overcome the sense of defeat and dispair by building for themselves a vital community."

He misspelled "despair." Nobody corrected it.

Ansel Adams, Manzanar War Relocation Center, 1943. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Cliff Palace, Mesa Verde National Park
posts.text:
600 cliff dwellings carved into sandstone in southern Colorado. The Ancestral Pueblo people lived here for 700 years, from 600 AD to 1300 AD. Then they left.

Nobody knows exactly why. Drought, maybe. Conflict. Something made an entire civilization walk away from cities they'd built into the side of a mountain.

In 1941, the National Park Service hired Ansel Adams to photograph the parks. He shot 226 images. The project was killed by World War II and never finished.

This is one of those 226.

Ansel Adams, Cliff Palace, Mesa Verde National Park, c. 1941. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Imogen Cunningham photographs Ansel Adams in Yosemite, 1953
posts.text:
America's most famous landscape photographer, photographed by America's most famous portrait photographer. In Yosemite. In 1953.

Imogen Cunningham was 70. She'd been shooting since 1901. Ansel Adams was 51, already a legend.

She caught him standing on a boulder with one arm raised, shot from below. He looks like he's conducting the valley.

Cunningham kept working until she died at 93. Her last project, never finished, was called "After Ninety."

Imogen Cunningham, Ansel Adams in Yosemite, 1953. Library of Congress.
